column1: Adult patient with acute head trauma L7Q4N2A9X5BC
column3:
Axial NECT, bone window shows a comminuted, depressed fracture of the left squamous temporal bone. There is marked swelling of the overlying scalp.

Axial NECT in the same patient shows the comminuted, depressed skull fracture is associated with an acute EDH (aEDH) . This classic EDH is hyperdense and has a typical biconvex, lens-shaped configuration. Note the focal subgaleal hematoma , indicating the impact site.

Coronal NECT in the same patient shows the aEDH as a biconvex collection causing local mass effect on the adjacent brain with adjacent sulcal effacement . aEDH was evacuated at surgery for the depressed skull fracture.
column5:
Epidural hematoma

Caption-derived keyword findings:
comminuted, depressed fracture
left squamous temporal bone
hyperdense
typical biconvex, lens-shaped configuration
focal subgaleal hematoma
local mass effect
adjacent sulcal effacement

Tight reasoning: The bone-window fracture plus hyperdense biconvex lens-shaped extraaxial collection at the impact site is the classic source-supported arterial EDH pattern. The coronal image confirms local mass effect without needing a clinical hint to solve the diagnosis.

Differential:
Acute subdural hematoma
Extraaxial neoplasm
Epidural empyema
column21:
🧠 Epidural Hematoma, Classic
Classic EDH is an extraaxial traumatic hemorrhage between skull and dura, classically hyperdense and biconvex/lentiform on noncontrast CT. The key board pivots are fracture/MMA injury, suture limitation with important exceptions, active-bleeding swirl/spot signs, venous sinus variants, and management triage.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis; Core Radiology Neuroimaging: Brain/Cerebral trauma pages labeled Neuro Brain 701-702, plus the fused RadPrimer + STATdx master source and STATdx selected image captions.

🔑 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • EDH = epidural hematoma.
  • aEDH = acute epidural hematoma.
  • MMA = middle meningeal artery.
  • CTA/CTV = CT angiography / CT venography.
📖 Definition / Mechanism
  • EDH is blood between the inner table of the skull and the outer periosteal dural layer; Core frames arterial EDH as blood external to the dura.
  • Classic traumatic arterial EDH is usually caused by fracture-related injury near the MMA groove, classically from squamous temporal bone fracture.
  • Venous EDH is less common and results from dural venous sinus laceration, especially near dural sinuses.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • CT: Best first-line trauma study is noncontrast head CT with bone windows; coronal/sagittal reformats are important for vertex or skull-base EDH.
  • Classic arterial pattern: hyperdense biconvex/lentiform extraaxial collection, often unilateral, supratentorial, temporoparietal, coup-site, adjacent to skull fracture, and usually limited by sutures.
  • Mass effect: EDH compresses underlying brain and subarachnoid space; large lesions risk herniation.
  • Active bleeding: mixed density or central low-density swirl sign suggests active/rapid bleeding with unretracted or unclotted blood; CTA spot/dot sign or contrast leakage predicts expansion.
  • MR: displaced dura can appear as a hypointense line between EDH and brain, helping distinguish EDH from SDH.
  • Air: air inside EDH suggests communication with paranasal sinus, mastoid air cells, or skull-base fracture; review adjacent bone windows.
⚖️ Boundary Rules / Exceptions
  • Arterial EDH usually does not cross sutures because dura is tightly adherent at sutures.
  • Exceptions include venous EDH, sutural diastasis/fracture, large hematoma with diastatic fracture, and pediatric cases.
  • Venous EDH may straddle sutures or dural attachments and can cross falx or tentorium; the dural sinus may be displaced or compressed rather than occluded.
  • Vertex EDH can be subtle and easily missed; coronal reconstructions and venous sinus evaluation are important.
🔍 Differential Diagnosis
  • Acute SDH is usually crescentic, crosses sutures, and is limited by dural reflections, but EDH and SDH can coexist and occasionally mimic each other.
  • Extraaxial neoplasm such as meningioma, lymphoma, or metastasis can mimic a biconvex epidural process when hemorrhagic or hyperdense.
  • Infection/inflammation, including epidural empyema, tuberculosis, and neurosarcoidosis, enters the differential in nontraumatic or chronic contexts.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • Classic lucid interval occurs in about half of cases but is not required.
  • Delayed development or enlargement is common within the first 36 hours; repeat CT is important when conservative management is chosen.
  • Most arterial EDHs are surgically evacuated, especially large, mixed-density, expanding, or mass-effect lesions.
  • Selected conservative management can be considered with GCS 13-15, EDH thickness below 40 mm, midline shift below 6 mm, no other surgical lesion, and close repeat imaging.
  • Some EDHs under 1 cm without edema may be managed nonoperatively; anterior middle cranial fossa venous EDHs are often observed.
  • Posterior fossa EDH carries higher mortality and may present later because venous expansion can be slower; posterior fossa mass effect raises urgency.
  • Endovascular MMA occlusion is a minimally invasive option in selected nonsurgical acute EDH cases.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Classic EDH = traumatic biconvex hyperdense extraaxial hematoma adjacent to fracture, usually MMA-related and suture-limited.
  • Do not overapply the suture rule: venous EDH, diastasis/fracture, large diastatic lesions, and pediatric cases can violate it.
  • Swirl/spot signs, expansion, mixed density, and mass effect are danger pivots; small observed EDH demands repeat imaging.

column1: Adult patient with acute head trauma P8C3D6M1K9JT
column2:
Initial NECT.

Repeat NECT 3 hours later.
column3:
Initial NECT.
Axial NECT shows an aEDH ; note focus of calcium displaced to the inner margin. Minimal mass effect was associated with the aEDH, so the patient was placed in the ER observation unit.

Repeat NECT 3 hours later.
Patient deteriorated 3 hours later. Repeat NECT shows growth of the EDH with a swirl sign . Surgery disclosed an actively bleeding hematoma between the periosteal and meningeal layers of the dura. Traumatic interdural hematomas are a rare variant, often associated with displaced dural calcifications.
column5:
Epidural hematoma (traumatic interdural variant)

Caption-derived keyword findings:
focus of calcium displaced to the inner margin
Minimal mass effect
deteriorated 3 hours later
growth
swirl sign
actively bleeding hematoma between the periosteal and meningeal layers of the dura
displaced dural calcifications

Tight reasoning: The initial extraaxial collection with displaced dural calcification followed by 3-hour enlargement and swirl sign matches the source-described rare traumatic interdural/EDH variant. The interval growth and active bleeding make this a danger-pattern EDH rather than a stable small collection.

Differential:
Acute subdural hematoma
Extraaxial neoplasm
Epidural empyema

column1: Adult patient with acute head trauma X5M2T8Q1N4PR
column3:
Axial NECT in a trauma patient shows an aEDH ; note the swirl sign and a focus of air within the hematoma, indicating a fractured paranasal sinus. Marked mass effect was associated with the hematoma, so the patient underwent surgery, which showed copious bleeding from a branch of the ethmoidal artery.

Axial bone CT of the same patient demonstrates a nondisplaced fracture of the right frontal sinus inner wall , which explains the focus of air within the aEDH.

swirl sign

Tight reasoning: The extraaxial hematoma has source-stated swirl sign and intralesional air, and the bone-window image shows the adjacent frontal sinus inner-wall fracture explaining the air. The combination supports EDH with an arterial/sinus-fracture source and mass-effect urgency.

Acute subdural hematoma
Extraaxial neoplasm
Epidural empyema

Initial axial NECT.
Axial NECT in a 47-year-old man with head trauma shows a classic biconvex (lentiform) uniformly hyperdense EDH in the right middle fossa. Bone CT (not shown) disclosed a nondisplaced skull fracture underlying the hematoma.

Initial coronal NECT.
Coronal NECT reformatted from the axial date nicely demonstrates the classic middle cranial fossa aEDH .

Initial sagittal NECT.
Sagittal NECT reformatted from the axial source date in the same patient shows the classic biconvex configuration of the middle fossa aEDH is present in all 3 major imaging planes.

NECT 5 weeks later.
NECT 5 weeks later shows the EDH is decreasing in size and density.

column3:
Initial NECT after acute head trauma.
NECT following acute head trauma shows a small right parietal aEDH . Only mild mass effect on the underlying brain and lateral ventricle is present. The patient's Glasgow Coma Scale was 13, so no surgery was performed.

Repeat scan 10 days later.
Repeat scan 10 days later shows the hematoma is unchanged in size, but its density has decreased significantly. Small bifrontal subdural hygromas are present. Repeat study 6 weeks after trauma (not shown) disclosed complete resolution of the EDH and subdural hygromas.

column1: Adult patient with acute head trauma L7Q4N2A9X5BC [DD1]
column3:
Axial NECT, bone window shows a comminuted, depressed fracture of the left squamous temporal bone. There is marked swelling of the overlying scalp.

Axial NECT in the same patient shows the comminuted, depressed skull fracture is associated with an acute EDH (aEDH) . This classic EDH is hyperdense and has a typical biconvex, lens-shaped configuration. Note the focal subgaleal hematoma , indicating the impact site.

Coronal NECT in the same patient shows the aEDH as a biconvex collection causing local mass effect on the adjacent brain with adjacent sulcal effacement . aEDH was evacuated at surgery for the depressed skull fracture.
column7: CT head. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9: Use the compartment boundary first: classic arterial EDH is biconvex/lentiform and usually suture-limited, while acute SDH is usually crescentic, crosses sutures, and is limited by falx/tentorium. column3: Coronal CTA head shows a venous EDH from a lacerated superior sagittal sinus. The dura and superior sagittal sinus are displaced away from the calvarium. column7: CTA head. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9: Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: the key discriminator is not simple suture limitation but venous sinus/dural displacement with a suture-crossing exception. Extraaxial neoplasm or epidural empyema should be favored only when the source-supported acute trauma/fracture/sinus-injury context is absent or chronic/nontraumatic.

column11: Why does classic arterial EDH form a biconvex collection that usually stops at sutures?
column12: A fracture-related arterial bleed, classically near the middle meningeal artery groove, accumulates external to the dura and peels the periosteal dural layer inward from the inner table. Because the dura is tightly adherent to the cranium at the sutures, the collection expands inward more than laterally across sutures, producing the classic lentiform/biconvex configuration. This mechanism explains why classic arterial EDH is usually suture-limited and adjacent to a skull fracture.

column11: Why does the CT swirl sign imply active or rapid bleeding in acute EDH?

column11: Why can venous EDH behave differently at sutures and dural attachments than classic arterial EDH?

The key imaging logic is to look for displacement or compression of the dural sinus/dura away from the skull, not to assume that suture crossing excludes EDH.

column11: Why does air inside an EDH point to a specific fracture-source search?
column12: Air within an EDH implies communication between the hematoma and an air-containing structure. The imaging consequence is practical: when gas is seen in an EDH, the adjacent bone-window images should be reviewed for a sinus, mastoid, or skull-base fracture explaining the communication.

column13: What is the trap in treating suture-crossing as an absolute rule for this entity?
column14:
Trap: Assuming that any collection crossing a suture must be subdural and cannot be EDH.
Safety check: Look for the source-supported exceptions: venous EDH, sutural diastasis/fracture, large hematoma with diastatic fracture, and pediatric cases.
Key discriminator: Classic arterial EDH usually stops at sutures, but venous EDH can straddle sutures or dural attachments and may cross falx/tentorium.
Why it happens: The classic arterial rule is true often enough to be useful, but overapplying it misses venous or diastatic EDH.

column13: What is the trap in dismissing a low-density component inside this entity?

Why it happens: Unclotted active blood is less dense than clotted acute blood, so danger can look deceptively hypodense.

column13: What is the trap when gas is seen inside this entity?
column14:
Trap: Treating intralesional air as incidental pneumocephalus and not searching for the fracture source.
Safety check: Review adjacent bone windows for paranasal sinus, mastoid air cell, or skull-base fracture communication.
Key discriminator: Source-supported EDH air suggests communication with an air-containing structure.
Why it happens: The air focus can draw attention away from the adjacent subtle bone-window abnormality that explains the hematoma source.
column21:
🧠 Epidural Hematoma, Classic
Classic EDH is an extraaxial traumatic hemorrhage between skull and dura, classically hyperdense and biconvex/lentiform on noncontrast CT. The key board pivots are fracture/MMA injury, suture limitation with important exceptions, active-bleeding swirl/spot signs, venous sinus variants, and management triage.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis; Core Radiology Neuroimaging: Brain/Cerebral trauma pages labeled Neuro Brain 701-702, plus the fused RadPrimer + STATdx master source and STATdx selected image captions.

🔑 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • EDH = epidural hematoma.
  • aEDH = acute epidural hematoma.
  • MMA = middle meningeal artery.
  • CTA/CTV = CT angiography / CT venography.
📖 Definition / Mechanism
  • EDH is blood between the inner table of the skull and the outer periosteal dural layer; Core frames arterial EDH as blood external to the dura.
  • Classic traumatic arterial EDH is usually caused by fracture-related injury near the MMA groove, classically from squamous temporal bone fracture.
  • Venous EDH is less common and results from dural venous sinus laceration, especially near dural sinuses.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • CT: Best first-line trauma study is noncontrast head CT with bone windows; coronal/sagittal reformats are important for vertex or skull-base EDH.
  • Classic arterial pattern: hyperdense biconvex/lentiform extraaxial collection, often unilateral, supratentorial, temporoparietal, coup-site, adjacent to skull fracture, and usually limited by sutures.
  • Mass effect: EDH compresses underlying brain and subarachnoid space; large lesions risk herniation.
  • Active bleeding: mixed density or central low-density swirl sign suggests active/rapid bleeding with unretracted or unclotted blood; CTA spot/dot sign or contrast leakage predicts expansion.
  • MR: displaced dura can appear as a hypointense line between EDH and brain, helping distinguish EDH from SDH.
  • Air: air inside EDH suggests communication with paranasal sinus, mastoid air cells, or skull-base fracture; review adjacent bone windows.
⚖️ Boundary Rules / Exceptions

  • Exceptions include venous EDH, sutural diastasis/fracture, large hematoma with diastatic fracture, and pediatric cases.
  • Venous EDH may straddle sutures or dural attachments and can cross falx or tentorium; the dural sinus may be displaced or compressed rather than occluded.
  • Vertex EDH can be subtle and easily missed; coronal reconstructions and venous sinus evaluation are important.

  • Acute SDH is usually crescentic, crosses sutures, and is limited by dural reflections, but EDH and SDH can coexist and occasionally mimic each other.

  • Infection/inflammation, including epidural empyema, tuberculosis, and neurosarcoidosis, enters the differential in nontraumatic or chronic contexts.

  • Classic lucid interval occurs in about half of cases but is not required.
  • Delayed development or enlargement is common within the first 36 hours; repeat CT is important when conservative management is chosen.
  • Most arterial EDHs are surgically evacuated, especially large, mixed-density, expanding, or mass-effect lesions.
  • Selected conservative management can be considered with GCS 13-15, EDH thickness below 40 mm, midline shift below 6 mm, no other surgical lesion, and close repeat imaging.
  • Some EDHs under 1 cm without edema may be managed nonoperatively; anterior middle cranial fossa venous EDHs are often observed.
  • Posterior fossa EDH carries higher mortality and may present later because venous expansion can be slower; posterior fossa mass effect raises urgency.
  • Endovascular MMA occlusion is a minimally invasive option in selected nonsurgical acute EDH cases.
⭐ Super Summary

column13: What is the trap after identifying one extraaxial hematoma on trauma CT?
column14:
Trap: Satisfaction of search after identifying EDH.
Safety check: Continue the trauma search pattern for SDH, SAH, and contusion.
Key discriminator: The sources state EDH can coexist with subdural hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or contusion; the CT comma sign indicates coexisting EDH and SDH.
Why it happens: The classic EDH pattern is visually satisfying, but coexisting compartments may require separate treatment planning.

column3: Axial bone CT in a young adult trauma patient demonstrates a subtle nondisplaced fracture of the right frontal bone and diastasis of the sagittal suture .
column13: What pitfall and safety check does this trauma image teach for this entity?
column14:
Trap: Missing a subtle fracture or sutural diastasis and then falsely assuming a vertex or venous EDH cannot cross a suture.
Safety check: Review bone windows and coronal/sagittal reformats when vertex EDH or venous sinus injury is possible.
Key discriminator: The source-supported exceptions include venous EDH and sutural diastasis/fracture; CTA/CTV can show displaced or compressed dural sinus.
Why it happens: A subtle bone-window abnormality can be easier to miss than the later extraaxial venous collection.

column15: What is the most common vascular mechanism for classic traumatic EDH?
column16:
Fact: Most EDHs are arterial; classic arterial EDH is usually from fracture-related injury near the middle meningeal artery groove / tearing of the middle meningeal artery.

column15: For acute EDH, what do the CT swirl sign and CTA spot/dot sign imply?
column16:
- Swirl sign: mixed density / central hypodense component from active or rapid bleeding with unretracted or unclotted blood.
- CTA spot/dot sign or active extravasation: contrast leakage.
- Contrast leakage predicts expansion.
Exam pivot: Mixed-density EDH is higher-risk than a uniform stable clot.

column15: When should CTA/CTV be considered in EDH evaluation?
column16:
- Suspected venous sinus injury or venous EDH near a dural sinus.

column15: How do anterior middle cranial fossa and posterior fossa venous EDHs differ in source-supported management concern?
column16:
- Anterior middle cranial fossa EDHs are nearly always venous, rarely expand, clinically indolent, and often observed.
- Posterior fossa venous EDHs may require surgical evacuation because of associated mass effect.
- STATdx notes posterior fossa EDH has higher mortality and may present later because venous expansion can be slower.
Exam pivot: Venous does not always mean low risk; location and mass effect matter.
column21:
🧠 Epidural Hematoma, Classic
Classic EDH is an extraaxial traumatic hemorrhage between skull and dura, classically hyperdense and biconvex/lentiform on noncontrast CT. The key board pivots are fracture/MMA injury, suture limitation with important exceptions, active-bleeding swirl/spot signs, venous sinus variants, and management triage.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer/STATdx synthesis; Core Radiology Neuroimaging: Brain/Cerebral trauma pages labeled Neuro Brain 701-702, plus the fused RadPrimer + STATdx master source and STATdx selected image captions.

🔑 Abbreviations / Acronyms
  • EDH = epidural hematoma.
  • aEDH = acute epidural hematoma.
  • MMA = middle meningeal artery.
  • CTA/CTV = CT angiography / CT venography.
📖 Definition / Mechanism
  • EDH is blood between the inner table of the skull and the outer periosteal dural layer; Core frames arterial EDH as blood external to the dura.
  • Classic traumatic arterial EDH is usually caused by fracture-related injury near the MMA groove, classically from squamous temporal bone fracture.
  • Venous EDH is less common and results from dural venous sinus laceration, especially near dural sinuses.
🖼️ Key Imaging Findings
  • CT: Best first-line trauma study is noncontrast head CT with bone windows; coronal/sagittal reformats are important for vertex or skull-base EDH.
  • Classic arterial pattern: hyperdense biconvex/lentiform extraaxial collection, often unilateral, supratentorial, temporoparietal, coup-site, adjacent to skull fracture, and usually limited by sutures.
  • Mass effect: EDH compresses underlying brain and subarachnoid space; large lesions risk herniation.
  • Active bleeding: mixed density or central low-density swirl sign suggests active/rapid bleeding with unretracted or unclotted blood; CTA spot/dot sign or contrast leakage predicts expansion.
  • MR: displaced dura can appear as a hypointense line between EDH and brain, helping distinguish EDH from SDH.
  • Air: air inside EDH suggests communication with paranasal sinus, mastoid air cells, or skull-base fracture; review adjacent bone windows.
⚖️ Boundary Rules / Exceptions
  • Arterial EDH usually does not cross sutures because dura is tightly adherent at sutures.
  • Exceptions include venous EDH, sutural diastasis/fracture, large hematoma with diastatic fracture, and pediatric cases.
  • Venous EDH may straddle sutures or dural attachments and can cross falx or tentorium; the dural sinus may be displaced or compressed rather than occluded.
  • Vertex EDH can be subtle and easily missed; coronal reconstructions and venous sinus evaluation are important.
🔍 Differential Diagnosis
  • Acute SDH is usually crescentic, crosses sutures, and is limited by dural reflections, but EDH and SDH can coexist and occasionally mimic each other.
  • Extraaxial neoplasm such as meningioma, lymphoma, or metastasis can mimic a biconvex epidural process when hemorrhagic or hyperdense.
  • Infection/inflammation, including epidural empyema, tuberculosis, and neurosarcoidosis, enters the differential in nontraumatic or chronic contexts.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • Classic lucid interval occurs in about half of cases but is not required.
  • Delayed development or enlargement is common within the first 36 hours; repeat CT is important when conservative management is chosen.
  • Most arterial EDHs are surgically evacuated, especially large, mixed-density, expanding, or mass-effect lesions.
  • Selected conservative management can be considered with GCS 13-15, EDH thickness below 40 mm, midline shift below 6 mm, no other surgical lesion, and close repeat imaging.
  • Some EDHs under 1 cm without edema may be managed nonoperatively; anterior middle cranial fossa venous EDHs are often observed.
  • Posterior fossa EDH carries higher mortality and may present later because venous expansion can be slower; posterior fossa mass effect raises urgency.
  • Endovascular MMA occlusion is a minimally invasive option in selected nonsurgical acute EDH cases.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Classic EDH = traumatic biconvex hyperdense extraaxial hematoma adjacent to fracture, usually MMA-related and suture-limited.
  • Do not overapply the suture rule: venous EDH, diastasis/fracture, large diastatic lesions, and pediatric cases can violate it.
  • Swirl/spot signs, expansion, mixed density, and mass effect are danger pivots; small observed EDH demands repeat imaging.

column15: After identifying an EDH on trauma CT, what source-supported search-pattern pivot remains important?
column16:
- EDH can coexist with SDH, SAH, or contusion.
- Continue the search pattern after finding the epidural collection.
- CT comma sign indicates coexisting EDH and SDH and may change treatment planning because compartments can require separate surgical planning.
Exam pivot: Do not stop at the first extraaxial hematoma.

column15: What does air within an EDH suggest, and what image review should follow?
column16:
- Air within EDH suggests communication with a paranasal sinus, mastoid air cells, or skull-base fracture.
- Review adjacent bone-window images.
Exam pivot: Gas in the hematoma is a fracture-source clue, not just incidental air.
